transcript.text: Hello, hello, Katia, how are you doing?
I'm good, how are you?
Is it Renato?
It's Renato or Ren, whatever you prefer.
Can you, you can hear me okay, right?
Yeah, I can hear you just fine.
I trouble adjusting my mic today.
Let's see.
Awesome.
Thank you so much for joining me.
We have half an hour.
How did your session with Bridget go?
It was great.
We were really crunched at the end.
We were talking quite a bit.
I didn't get to ask so many questions.
I didn't want to be, she didn't want me to be late to this one.
I'm going to make sure that I leave a bunch of time towards the end for you to ask me everything that you want as well.
And I have no problem going over either.
If we have to, let me check if that's correct.
Yes.
That's fully correct.
Awesome.
Let's see.
Let me introduce myself first, Katia.
I mean, it's like we've had you through the system, so I've scoped out your stuff.
About me, I have been a designer for a long time.
I started my career as a designer doing graphic design, then moving into interaction design.
I founded a couple of companies.
Then I became an operator in tech and started leading large design and product and marketing orgs.
And I did that for Lyft, for Grammarly, Honor, a bunch of other companies.
And now, yeah, the industry has changed significantly.
So it's back to work for everybody.
And yeah.
At GrowthX, there is a level of complexity and velocity and opportunity that was impossible to say no to.
So I have been enjoying myself for the past whopping month onboarding here and learning my way around things.
But it's very clear that we need many more hands to do the work that we want to do.
So you've read some of it about it already.
still like a role.
It's quite in flux.
You know, as with everything at GrowthX, it's in flux.
It's still very early stage.
But I'd love to focus this interview a little bit on or a couple of minutes on taste, craft, maybe precision.
I want to do a little bit of systems thinking, philosophy, and a couple of questions around team.
And...
And...
A collaboration, and from there, I just want to leave it open for you to ask me everything.
So feel free to time box your answers.
And I was like, if we keep everything in 15 to 20 minutes, you know, it's like we should have plenty of time for more open-ended questions from your end.
Sound good?
Yep, sounds good.
Awesome.
Perfect.
All right.
How would you describe your own personal design taste?
Like maybe you can share an example of work that really reflects that taste.
You can refer to it.
You don't have to show it.
Okay.
I honestly, I'm pretty into neo-brutalism design.
I don't get to do a lot of that stuff in day-to-day work.
But I'm really into kind of that old retro, futuristic vibe.
kind of, I don't know if you've seen a lot of work.
I have one article.
This in my mind, or designer in my mind, named Yoann Putra.
Very kind of like Neo-Tokyo design.
I feel like in an ideal world, that is the type of work that I would like to do personally.
But honestly, I'm kind of like, I try not to like fall for trends too easily because they shift so fast, especially now.
So I'm really just in flux with how I'm feeling out.
I'm keeping an eye out on what the industry is doing, what folks are doing, but also just trying not to let myself fall into that bubble of trends too.
That's awesome.
Can you spell Yoann Putra for me?
Y-O-A-N and then space P-U-T-R-A.
They are coincidentally also an Indonesian designer.
I was also like, oh, this is kind of like a kinship.
Yeah, that's very cool.
You are...
You have Balinese heritage, correct?
Yes.
Very, very cool.
Oh, wow.
This is super, super, super cool.
Yeah, it's super fun.
And it's been making me really want to dive into 3D a bit more.
dabble in it, but I would really like to get more into it, to do things like that.
Cool, cool.
Thank you for sharing that.
What details do you obsess over that others may overlook?
Hmm, sorry, say that last part again.
That others may overlook.
Like maybe you can walk me through a time where putting in some extra effort or sweating the details really made like the final work.
It's like 10 times better.
Yeah, for sure.
For example, and this is work that I have up on my portfolio, but I did a lot of content for Meta AI.
And so I worked on that account for about a year and a half.
I led the design.
And so that meant...
We have like over 200 pieces of content.
I guarantee you I've touched every single one of them.
And with a product, with promoting a product that's obviously so new, and at that time it was during the AI boom, it was kind of, I had to really figure out how to design.
We were doing a lot of social concepts, so that would look like content with creators and influencers, but then we'd also have to figure out how to show the product in an easy and quick way.
And so I had to pay a lot of attention to the UI and UX of how our product, the meta product was changing, and a lot of people could easily miss.

It's changing all the time, and a lot of people have missed it, but I know by the end of the video production, I need to make sure that it's accurate to what's actually live on the platform so that when we post this and people see it at that moment.
But in time, it's at least, it's matching the current UI.
And so I did a lot of like investigative work and like making sure that everything, I should also preface that we at the time did not have access to UI files from Meta.
And so I had to go in and recreate and reanimate things on my own.
So it was really making sure everything was fine to the like grain of sand.
Because obviously, like if I'm mimicking UI products and I don't have actual files, it has to be almost one-to-one.
Fantastic.
Really good detail.
That's the type of gritty stuff that, you know, it's like teaches you hard lessons sometimes.
When do you know that something's done?
When does it feel done to you?
Oh, I, hmm.
Hmm, okay.
For one example, I have been working on a pretty big Anthem video.
Hmm.
Brand Anthem video currently.
And there's obviously a lot of stakeholders.
When you're working with people promoting themselves, there's going to be a lot of stakeholders.
And that's the preface of the video right now.
It's about the company and about the team.
And so there's obviously a lot of people in the mix, and I know everybody needs to get their eyes on it.
I feel like a project isn't, there's two sides to this coin.
For me, I feel like a project isn't done until I feel like I'm proud to put it up on my portfolio.

And on the other side, obviously a project isn't done until I've made sure that all the stakeholders, all the people that need to get their word in, get their word into me so I can make any final edits or anything like that.
But above all, for me as a designer, as a creative, I really value in making sure that I feel good about a project.
I've done that I can stick it up on my portfolio because obviously that's what represents my skills, my mindset, and my talent as a whole.
Thank you.
Let me see.
Tell me maybe about a time where you protected the visual bar, when your team maybe wanted to move a little faster than you do.
As in like workload?
Is that what you mean?
No, perhaps the quality was not at a point yet where you felt comfortable with it.
And perhaps the team wanted to move a little faster.
Have you ever been in a situation like that?
Yeah, I feel like it's very rare because a lot of the teams I've worked with really emphasize the quality over anything else.
but that said, working with such like large tech giants like ...
We've worked with Microsoft.
I've worked with Nike before.
Their needs are always changing and so are their asks.
And with that, that means tight deadlines, super tight deadlines.
And there was a few times where I was live on the ground at Meta Connect 2023.
I was part of the team that was pumping out live content during the event where everyone was so exhausted.
It was like a 12-hour workday and we had like one or two last pieces of content that needed to get out.
But I was just firmly sitting at my desk.
I made sure I had conversations with my creative directors and art directors at that time and like walked with them through the possible alternatives to edits to a video.
It's like a 10-second video, but we knew that it meant so much to the team.
The client team.
And obviously, we're getting it out live the day of.
It had to be done.

And so, really, what I had to do, because I was a designer for the project, I had to really sit down and make sure that my team felt fully aware of what the concept was going to end up looking like and making sure that we are happy as a team and also making sure that the client is appreciative of what we've done.
So, really, just managing also kind of team expectations and making sure we're doing quality assurance throughout the process.
Cool, cool, cool.
Let's see.
So, historically, what types of designers or systems or visual movements shaped your bar for quality?
Yeah, I feel like I'm sorry.
Can you ask that question again?
Yeah, of course, of course, of course.
And I was like, what historical or, you know, it's like perhaps design education, like your foundations in design education, right?
It's like what designers or systems or, you know, like frameworks perhaps or specific visual styles have shaped your bar for quality?
Yes, I am so blanking out on his actual name.
There was someone, I think it may have been, it starts with a J, but he was the one who made the principles of grids.
And it's like that massive book of like grid systems and like understanding how to keep things looking neat and laid out and how that transfers across.
I don't know.
I feel so ashamed.
Miller Brockman.
Yes, I think that's it.
Yes, he had like really like infamous book on grids.
And it's obviously been a few years.
It was when I studied at my college that we were really looking through these texts and obviously the fundamentals.
And obviously nowadays, like as a designer, I'm evolving in a way where I'm just doing things as I go.
But I really value where design has started because obviously without that kind of route or that point of start, we wouldn't have a lot of the thinking that we have today.

Like it's ingrained, like you don't think about it that often as a designer, but some things are ingrained in you, like how you're treating layouts and all that stuff, how you're approaching a project, your mentality.
I had a lot of, I feel like that grid, that book was a good starting point for me in my initial studies.
So, yeah.
Oh, cool.
Yeah.
It's an oldie for sure, but.
Yeah, yeah, I know it well.
Have you ever helped build or contribute to perhaps like a design system or a tooling workflow?
Yes, I was talking to Bridget about this because I know the role is basically will be that.
Obviously, the AI boom is really just, it's obviously not really just started, but it's really started to shift in the creative, it started to make shifts in the creative industry.

I guess before all of the AI platforms and AI softwares that we get to use these days, a lot of my working in an agency, especially such a small team working on so many big clients, I'm always trying to find ways to make efficiency in my workflow, whether that's templatizing in After Effects or making layout sizes in Illustrator.
After a lot of my upbringing, I guess upbringing here.
In agency world was kind of related to that.
was obviously we're not looking to have to continuously do the same thing over and over again if we can find a way to make it a bit easier, more efficient on the team.
So that has been something that I've done before, just not in like the AI or like really the tech sense, since that was pretty, that kind of shift is pretty new to agency.
No worries at all, no worries at all.
How do you feel about designing tools or systems that automate some of your own design work?
Yeah, I mean, I'm always of the mind that if I could find a way to make my workflow a little easier and maybe a little bit, again, a little bit more efficient, that I'm all game for that.
And at the end of the day, I love being kind of, when I like imagine myself, let's say in this role or doing these kinds
So projects or types of work where I'm helping build a system like this, I obviously would continue to still pioneer design and how things look.
But being able to have these tools that help us kind of give back to our clients a little bit faster and maybe a little bit better, I'm obviously open to that.
And that's also like a larger, my larger philosophy right now, as we're obviously the creative industry is a little tumultuous right now.
I feel like everybody has mixed opinions about AI tools and all that stuff.
And I'm trying to stand my ground.
And I firmly believe that, you know, we have to use these tools.
They're there.
Why wouldn't we use these tools to better ourselves?
Like the 20%, the top 20% of the people in our fields or in any of the fields will know how to use these tools better than the 80% and that's how they're, they're up there, they're at the top.
Their game.
That's how I feel personally.
That's an excellent segue into my next question.
What kind of designer are you trying to become?
Yeah, I mean, I am at that point in my career where I feel like I've dabbled a lot in everything I've done.
I'm really big on motion design right now, really big on video production, but I also have history with UI UX branding.
You've seen my portfolio.
I like to kind of call myself a unicorn, almost a unicorn.
I'm obviously still wanting to learn new things and new ways to better my skill set, whether that's things like 3D or learning to vibe code or in working in Webflow and stuff like that.
I'm really just looking to continue to be that unicorn.
Like I really don't want to stifle myself in a specific type of role.
And so I'm looking for a challenge and a segue in my career to really learn and...
Focus around these new tools that are available, tools that your team is building, because that's, that is quite literally the future, it's the present, actually.
So, like, really pushing myself to be, like, a Neo designer and not being afraid to leverage those tools.
So, yeah.
Very cool.
Neo designer.
Let's see.
Tell me about a time where your personal taste didn't align with a team or brand.
How did you handle that?
Oh, yeah.
did.
We did, at my current role, we did a brand refresh, actually, pretty recently.
And so, it was not really a competition, but it was three of the designers kind of going against each other.
But obviously, we weren't, like, we weren't actually, like, oh, my gosh, I want my design.
We were working amongst one another and, like, sharing our ideas.
And so obviously the hardest people to impress is your own team because it's so difficult to impress your own CEOs or anything like that.
And I shared some new designs that were a huge branch away, not huge of a branch away from our previous branding, but enough that it felt different.
Enough that it invoked thoughts in other people and thinking, is this a departure or is this close enough to what we have?

And I think how they describe my concept in between the three designers is that I was like the safe medium where I was still poking out the boundary, but also still sticking true to like previous brand guidelines, whether that was like color or typography.
And then the other designers was like the way out of bounds and then really safe.
And so that was.
A recent moment, guess.
wasn't that recent, but maybe a year ago.
And I mean, obviously, we're doing this work for ourselves.
There's no harm done in receiving feedback.
And in the end, I made a lot of adjustments to my concept and the brand design.
And we ended up really coalescing what everybody came up with and coming up with our current brand system.
But it's always, you know, it's always, it's always interesting when you get feedback from, I've had my, I've had my VP of design come to me and be like, I hate this gradient.
And I'm like, okay, so it happens, but no harm done.
And there's always like reasoning behind those like brand decisions or those visual decisions.
And I'm always just, feedback is what feeds me, feeds me.
So, yeah.
Thank you so much, Katia.
What can I answer for you?
Yeah.
Yeah, so many questions.
I hope I don't have too many.
Renato, go ahead.
Obviously, you are a principal designer.
I'm really, really curious about what that role really means to you and what your day-to-day looks like.
Yeah, let's see.
What does that mean to me?
Well, it's a principal designer.
Everybody at GrowthX currently on the EPD side, I would say it's like anybody who's doing any type of work, really, because the team is so small and so tight-knit, is an individual contributor.
So meaning that 50% of the time you'll spend thinking things through, talking things through, and the other 50% you try to produce things that actually put that thinking into effect somehow.
I think with GrowthX.
GrowthX.
Being fully AI native on all fronts requires us to think completely different about how we run, how we grow the company, how we run individual processes, but also how we run collaborative processes.
We're mostly remote, however, there are sync parts to our company, there are hybrid parts to our company, and there's a lot of fluidity in general.
As a leading individual contributor, what do I do?
My purview right now is as broad as it can be, and I was like, this is literally zero to one startup, even though on the deliverance services front, we are booking a lot of success with clients.
You know, so on the software side, we have plenty of miles to cover.
We actually have some catching up.
You know, it's like the services that we're, that we're offering.
So from that perspective, you know, it's like the, the more speed we can maintain, the better.

So I'm, yeah, it's like interfacing throughout the, throughout the company, looking at how people in Deliver operate, looking at how people in Build, which is the, you know, like where we build the software side of the, side of the company operates.
Right.
I've been contributing here and there to, you it's like projects in flight where, where necessary.
It's only been, you know, it's like a month or so for me onboarding.
So it is still, you know, it's like trying to understand and develop a stronger opinion of that, you know, it's like the type of design that is necessary right now.
And, you know, the types of folks and roles that connect to that on the longer term.
So, but that's, you know, it's like slowly, but surely it's like all falling into place.
Um, and then the team is, uh, is.
But yeah, I would say my role as a principal designer right now is 30% research, 30% execution, and then 30% finding the right folks for the job.
Is the connection okay?
On my end, let's see.
I still hear you.
No issues.
Let me see.
Maybe turning off a video improves it.
How's that?
Yeah, I can hear you just fine.
Okay.
It looks like it says that it's on your end for me, though.
It might be.
I'm not 100.
You can hear me just fine?
Yeah, I can hear you.
Okay.
All right.
I think we're okay now.
Sorry about that.
No worries.
Well, I mean, I talked to Bridget earlier, and she was kind of in that same mind space, too, do that.
Obviously, you guys are kind of in that tech startup space, and the roles are shifting, and I know that's probably what's happening.
Well, that's definitely what's happening with the current role that I'm applying for.
But yeah, that sounds, okay, that's pretty interesting to hear.
It's definitely such a new environment, it sounds like, at GrowthX for designers.
So yeah, super interesting.
I guess that segues into my next question, is that I've obviously heard quite a bit from the team about, and Bridget, too, about this role specifically.

But I'm just curious how you envision this person, or me, to, when I, let's say, when I join, what my day-to-day would look like, or who I'd interact with, and also, maybe who are the other team members for the design front?
Yeah, absolutely.
So the other team members on the design front are still, it looks like very, it's very limited still, it's myself.
It's George Romain, a new product design engineer that just onboarded, and a third member that will be joining a couple of weeks from now.
They will be spending most of their time on the build side, contributing directly to code.
Yeah.
However, there will be plenty of collaboration throughout, I would say.
And the closer that this role, this spend to the build side, the better, of course, the work on the delivery side will be.

But from a day-to-day perspective, you will be mostly covering or supporting the entire deliver org, which means all the pods that are delivering services directly to clients have some types of design needs.
And these design needs range from on-the-spot art direction or creative direction for specific projects.
So marketing managers on these pods sometimes will create graphics themselves or do a first pass.
They are in need of quality control.
So there's a lot of that happening throughout.
So one of the challenges there is being able to delegate and communicate visual feedback very clearly across different levels of seniority.
Then, let's see, in addition to that, there is producing assets.
Now, that was a bit of a push and pull and a bit of a calibration in terms of what are the most important projects?
What are the highest priority things to focus on?
Generally, these are mapped right now by size of account.
And...

It's like the overall heat of a certain client, but these could be producing things similar to the assignment you did to fully flushed out web pages to any types of visual assets that you can imagine within brand and marketing design, essentially.
So those are really the production layers, I would say.

The third component, really, where the biggest strategic area for this role is, and in my opinion, when I look at the entire design industry, it's like the biggest opportunity for growth is to consider all of the work that is happening within the organization.
And as you contribute, as we calibrate, as we make that work.
think I think so.
think

We, you know, like do some pattern recognition and see, it's like, hey, where can we improve things by applying workflows, either through using our own systems or our own software, or using, you know, like different types of software, but then documenting that proper workflow in a similar way so that it can be reproduced by folks who, you know, may be part of the design team, may not be part of the design team, or essentially thinking about like how we can do that and how we can do the most repetitive, automatable things at scale.
So there's that, and I think critically about that so that, you know, it's like we can scale the majority of the design strength that you have as much as possible across pods.
So those are, I think it's like the biggest conceptual challenges within the role.
Yeah, okay, that, that seems.
Align with what I was imagining too.
again, Bridget and I were really talking about this role being kind of like quality assurance or really helping come up with new ways to improve the system.
So definitely not something that I'm new to entirely in IDEA, but again, new in terms of like the actual like software or like product that I'm using.
So, yeah, that seems pretty, pretty much what I was thinking.
We are, we are over time.
I do have one more question, if that's okay with you.
Go right ahead.
Okay.

My, my final question is that I'm just curious how as the company grows and as this software continues to develop and let's say, and as this role is continuing to help make these changes to your products, how does the design team, how could it change or how can it be affected later down in the line?
Yeah, it's a really good question.
I think, you know, for.
For the design industry as a whole, I think there's, and this doesn't just count for the design industry, would say, Katia.
I've led, you know, as a product organizations, research organizations, I've managed engineering teams.
Essentially, it's like any role that contributes to a product or service development process in some way, know, it's like that touches production.
It is affected by everything that we're building here.
And as a result, the entire functions are affected, right?
It's like including the structures that generally kept a certain rhythm and cadence to the product development process, right?
Right now, because we have access to all of these tools, it's becoming more like a way of communicating rather than...

In addition to shipping things faster, meaning that for designers in particular, it has both increased the quality of inputs that you get from everybody, but it also increased the noise to a certain extent.

So it's more and more important to have folks that understand what quality is, what quality looks like, how to push for that bar and how to communicate that bar, how to educate that bar specifically to others.
So that's how I see just the general design field evolving.
And indeed what you say, you know, I can have more focus on an opera segment that is good at communicating and delegating specifically, more so than...
So...
So...
So...
Being, for example, like really fast at utilizing a specific tool.
That translates to growthx specifically.

Like I said, we're fully AI native, meaning that we tend to think differently about how we handle headcount, overall structures and whatnot, hoping that we stay as small as possible for as long as possible, right?
Meaning on the software front, it's like, we don't want to get bigger than like 20 folks in the next two years.
On the deliver front, we also don't want to keep hiring as fast as we're doing right now to make sure that we scale what we currently have.
So there, we still need to figure that out.
But, you know, like with designing folks like me and the folks that are coming in, my...
Gut feeling about whether they appreciate design on long-term, it's pretty solid, right?
It's like, I think there's a certain, there's definitely like a certain undertone of like uncertainty throughout the industry about all of our roles.

But in terms of growth path, I think there's both, you know, like the experience that I and, you like some of the folks that we've hired bring along and what they've encountered in their career, building fantastic products.
But at the same time, you know, like we have a lot of control over how we shape growth paths for the future, right?
And joining a company like GrowthX, you know, building from zero to one, joining early on the design team, being creative.
You have a lot of opportunity to influence that whole process and say, and it's like, hey, how can I contribute to shaping what a great design path looks like for?
The future of brand and marketing design roles.
Awesome.
Thank you.
That was really cool here.
I appreciate that.
Fantastic.
Well, if you have any more questions, feel free to email.
We're, of course, going to do our sync up and get back to ASAP.
Okay.
It was wonderful talking to you, Katia.
I had a great time speaking to you and Bridget both.
This was really fun.
Thank you so much for your time.
Thank you.
Likewise.
Have a good one.
Bye-bye.
too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Katia Pembayun for Visual Designer, Content Automation role at GrowthX.ai](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Katia has diverse design experience, with a focus on motion design and video production](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=75.0)
  - [GrowthX is seeking designers who can adapt to AI-driven workflows and improve efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)
  - [The role involves quality control, asset production, and developing scalable design systems](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=140.0)
  - [GrowthX aims to stay lean while scaling, emphasizing adaptability and strategic thinking in design](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=191.0)

## Topics

### Katia's Design Background and Preferences

  - [Interested in neo-brutalism and retro-futuristic design (e.g., Yoann Putra's work)](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=221.0)
  - [Extensive experience with social content creation for major tech companies (Meta AI, Microsoft, Nike)](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=344.0)
  - [Values efficiency and creating reusable design systems/templates](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=225.0)
  - [Considers herself a "unicorn" designer with skills across multiple disciplines](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1028.0)

### GrowthX Design Team Structure and Expectations

  - [Small, tight-knit team currently consisting of Renato, George Romain, and one upcoming hire](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1551.0)
  - [Roles are fluid, with a 50/50 split between strategic thinking and hands-on production](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1278.0)
  - [Seeking designers who can adapt to AI-native workflows and improve efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1991.0)
  - [Focus on quality control, asset production, and developing scalable design systems](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1704.0)

### Role Responsibilities and Challenges

  - [Supporting the entire deliver org across multiple client pods](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1603.0)
  - [Providing on-the-spot art direction and creative direction](https://fathom.video/share/g7zKKH5rXsKi19NPCFxb5b5tR13x74nx?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)
